title: ⚠ MAP KEY — these are MODERN political borders
description: All coloured regions on this map are MODERN country borders (post-1945). They do NOT represent historical empires, dynasties (Tang, Ming, Qing), colonial territories (British Raj, Dutch East Indies, Ceylon as distinct from Sri Lanka), or pre-modern geography. Shown to orient students to where tea is produced today.

China (modern borders) — origin + largest producer
description: Modern political border (post-1949). ORIGIN of Camellia sinensis. Lu Yu's 'Cha Jing' (760 CE) defines tea as philosophical practice. Yunnan + Fujian provinces still anchor production; 46% of world supply today.

title: Japan (modern borders) — cultural elevation of tea
description: Modern political border. Chanoyu ('the way of tea'). Sen no Rikyū (1522–1591) codifies wabi-sabi aesthetic. Matcha culture from Buddhist monks. Production today is small but influence is global.

India (Assam + Darjeeling + Nilgiris tea zones)
description: Modern political border (post-1947). Three distinct tea zones: Darjeeling (high-altitude Himalayan foothills, muscatel flavour), Assam (tropical lowlands, bold malty), Nilgiris (south Indian highlands). Plantations built on British-era indentured labour (Sharma 2011); 23% of world production.

title: Sri Lanka (modern borders) — Ceylon highlands
description: Modern political border (post-1972; 'Ceylon' is the colonial-era name still used on tea tins). British converted coffee plantations to tea after 1869 coffee-rust epidemic wiped out the coffee industry. Central Highlands at ~4,000 ft produce bright citrusy Orange Pekoe.

title: Kenya (modern borders) — world's largest tea exporter by volume
description: Modern political border (post-1963 independence). Rift Valley highlands produce 8% of world tea. Kenya is the world's LARGEST tea exporter by volume — most of your average supermarket tea-bag blend comes from here.

Turkey (modern borders) — Black Sea tea
description: Modern political border (post-1923). Rize Province on the Black Sea coast. Turkey is among the top 5 world consumers per-capita despite being a minor producer — tea became national drink in the 20th century.
